# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions test **Bloom's Level 1 (Remember)** and **Level 2 (Understand)** cognitive outcomes. Direct, definition-style questions.

### Q1. Define Programmable System on Chip (PSoC). List any four of its distinguishing features compared to a conventional microcontroller. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Programmable System on Chip (PSoC)** is a single integrated circuit that combines a microcontroller CPU core with user-configurable analog and digital peripheral blocks, programmable interconnect, and on-chip memory, allowing the system designer to create custom peripheral functions in hardware.

**Four distinguishing features:**

1. **Configurable analog blocks** — op-amps, ADCs, DACs, comparators that can be re-wired dynamically.
2. **Universal Digital Blocks (UDBs)** — PLD + datapath-based blocks that synthesize custom digital peripherals.
3. **Programmable routing** — analog global bus and digital HSIOM matrix allow flexible signal routing.
4. **Mixed-signal integration** — analog and digital resources coexist on a single die, reducing external components.

---

### Q2. What is a Universal Digital Block (UDB) in a PSoC? Mention its three main internal sub-blocks. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Universal Digital Block (UDB)** is a programmable digital building block inside a PSoC that can be configured to implement custom digital peripherals (PWM, UART, SPI, CRC, state machines) via hardware description.

**Three main sub-blocks of a UDB:**

1. **PLD macrocell** — an AND-OR programmable logic array for combinational and registered logic.
2. **Datapath module** — an 8-bit ALU with FIFOs, CRC logic, and status flags for sequential data manipulation.
3. **Control/status registers** — 8-bit configuration and observation registers that interface the UDB to the CPU bus.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions test **Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)** cognitive outcomes. Internal choice is mandatory. The pattern below is exactly what appears in the KTU question paper.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Programmable System on Chip (PSoC). Describe the role of each major subsystem. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the internal architecture of a Universal Digital Block (UDB) with the help of a block diagram. How does it differ from a standard FPGA logic block? **(7 marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Q. (a) Model Answer

The architecture of a PSoC consists of **five major subsystems**, as illustrated in the following block diagram (see **Section 4.1** for the detailed Mermaid diagram).

**1. CPU Subsystem (4 marks for the block diagram + 1 mark for the explanation):**
The CPU subsystem is the master controller. It is typically an **ARM Cortex-M0, M0+, M3, or M4** core (in modern PSoCs) or an **8051** (in PSoC 3). It executes firmware stored in Flash, manages memory access via the bus matrix, and configures all other subsystems through memory-mapped registers.

**2. Memory Subsystem (1 mark):**
Contains **Flash** (non-volatile firmware), **SRAM** (runtime data), and **ROM** (bootloader and device-specific calibration).

**3. Programmable Digital Subsystem — UDB Array (1 mark):**
Multiple UDBs are tiled in a row and connected to a digital routing fabric. Each UDB can synthesize a custom peripheral.

**4. Programmable Analog Subsystem (1 mark):**
A library of analog blocks (op-amps, SAR ADC, $\Delta\Sigma$ ADC, DACs, comparators, CapSense) that can be interconnected through the analog routing bus.

**5. Programmable Routing & I/O (1 mark):**
The **analog global bus**, **analog local bus**, **digital routing fabric**, and **HSIOM** matrix allow signals to flow between any combination of blocks and to physical pins.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
This partitioning makes the PSoC a "system on chip" — not just a CPU — by integrating processing, configurable analog, configurable digital, and interconnect in a single die.

---

### Q. (b) Model Answer

**Universal Digital Block (UDB) Internal Architecture:**

A UDB consists of **three main sub-blocks** (see **Section 4.2** Mermaid diagram):

**1. PLD Macrocells (3 marks):**
Each UDB contains **two PLD macrocells**. Each macrocell has:
- An **AND-OR programmable logic array** with **12 product-term inputs** and **4 output terms**.
- A **configurable output flip-flop** (D, T, JK, or SR mode).
- The PLD synthesizes **combinational and sequential logic** from Boolean expressions or Verilog code.

**2. Datapath Module (2 marks):**
An 8-bit wide ALU that supports arithmetic (ADD, SUB, INC, DEC), logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT), and shift operations. It includes:
- **Two 4-byte FIFOs** for buffered data transfer.
- **CRC computation** logic.
- **Status flags** (zero, carry, overflow, MSB detected).

**3. Control/Status Registers (1 mark):**
8-bit configuration and observation registers that allow the CPU to read state and control UDB operation. Interrupt generation is also supported.

**Difference from FPGA Logic Block (1 mark):**

| Aspect | UDB (PSoC) | FPGA Logic Block (LUT-based) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Coarse (PLD + datapath) | Fine (LUT + flip-flop) |
| Purpose | Peripheral synthesis | General logic |
| Datapath | Built-in ALU, FIFO | Fabricated from LUTs |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A UDB is **coarser and more specialized** than an FPGA logic block — optimized for synthesizing peripherals rather than arbitrary logic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

> **Q. (a)** Describe the programmable analog subsystem of a PSoC. Explain the operation of a SAR ADC and derive the expression for its quantization step voltage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A PSoC's $\Delta\Sigma$ ADC samples an audio signal of bandwidth $f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$ at an oversampling ratio of 128. Compute (i) the sampling frequency, (ii) the resolution gain, and (iii) the resulting output resolution assuming a 1-bit first-order modulator. **(7 marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Q. (a) Model Answer

**Programmable Analog Subsystem Overview (3 marks):**

The programmable analog subsystem of a PSoC consists of:
- **Configurable op-amps** (4 typically) — can operate as inverting, non-inverting, follower, or comparator.
- **SAR ADC** — successive-approximation, 12-bit, up to 1 Msps.
- **$\Delta\Sigma$ ADC** — oversampling noise-shaping, up to 20-bit.
- **DACs** — current-output (IDAC) and voltage-output (VDAC).
- **Comparators** with programmable hysteresis.
- **CapSense** — touch-sensing hardware using CSD (CapSense Sigma Delta) technique.
- **Analog routing** — AG (global) and AL (local) buses for signal routing.

These blocks are connected via a programmable analog routing fabric and can be **re-wired at runtime** through configuration registers.

**SAR ADC Operation (2 marks):**

A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** performs binary-search quantization:
1. The input voltage $V_{in}$ is held on a sample-and-hold capacitor.
2. The SAR register starts at the **MSB = 1** and all other bits = 0, producing a candidate DAC voltage $V_{DAC}$.
3. The comparator checks whether $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$.
4. If true, the MSB remains 1; if false, it is cleared to 0.
5. The process repeats for the next bit, descending toward the LSB.
6. After $N$ clock cycles, the SAR register contains the $N$-bit digital representation of $V_{in}$.

**Derivation of Quantization Step Voltage (2 marks):**

For an $N$-bit SAR ADC with full-scale input range $V_{FS}$, the number of discrete output codes is:

$$L = 2^N$$

The voltage difference between adjacent codes is the **LSB voltage** (quantization step):

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$$

For example, for a 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 3.3\,\text{V}$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV/LSB}$$

---

### Q. (b) Model Answer

**Given:**
- Signal bandwidth: $f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$
- Oversampling ratio: $OSR = 128$

**(i) Sampling Frequency (3 marks):**

Using the oversampling relationship:

$$f_s = 2 \cdot f_{max} \cdot OSR$$

Substituting values:

$$f_s = 2 \times 20{,}000 \times 128 = 5{,}120{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 5.12\,\text{MHz}$$

**(ii) Resolution Gain (2 marks):**

For a first-order $\Delta\Sigma$ modulator, the resolution gain is:

$$\Delta N = 0.5 \cdot \log_2(OSR) = 0.5 \times \log_2(128) = 0.5 \times 7 = 3.5 \text{ bits}$$

**(iii) Output Resolution (2 marks):**

A 1-bit first-order $\Delta\Sigma$ modulator has a base resolution of 1 bit. Adding the gain:

$$N_{out} = 1 + \Delta N = 1 + 3.5 = 4.5 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** A first-order 1-bit modulator with OSR=128 can only achieve ~4.5 bits. To reach 16-bit audio resolution, PSoC devices use **higher-order modulators (L=3 or L=4)** with the same OSR, achieving 16-20 effective bits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on PSoC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PSoC with FPGA** — A PSoC is *microcontroller-class*; an FPGA has *no built-in CPU or analog*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Omitting the routing fabric** — A common mistake is describing only the CPU + UDB + Analog blocks. The **programmable interconnect** is what makes it a *system on chip*. Always include it.
> 3. **Wrong formula for ADC LSB** — Students write $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ instead of $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$. The **$2^N - 1$** is the correct denominator (number of *intervals* between codes).
> 4. **Ignoring the "P" in PSoC** — The word *Programmable* is critical. If your answer describes fixed peripherals, you have described a *traditional MCU*, not a PSoC. Examiners expect explicit mention of **user-configurability**.
> 5. **Mixing up UDB and PLD** — The UDB contains *two PLD macrocells plus a datapath*. Don't describe only the PLD part; the **datapath is half the UDB's power**.
> 6. **No mention of the development tool** — KTU examiners expect the student to know that PSoC is designed using **PSoC Creator** or **ModusToolbox** IDEs. Mentioning this tool adds a mark.
> 7. **Forgetting the differential amplifier** — PSoC's analog subsystem supports a **fully differential amplifier** mode, not just single-ended. Examiners love testing this.
> 8. **Confusing CapSense and ADC** — CapSense is a *touch-sensing* hardware that *internally* uses a $\Delta\Sigma$ modulator, but it is not the same as a general-purpose ADC.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **PSoC = Programmable System on Chip** — CPU + configurable analog + configurable digital + configurable routing.
- ✅ **Developed by Cypress Semiconductor (now Infineon Technologies)** — established in 2002 (PSoC 1).
- ✅ **CPU Core:** ARM Cortex-M (M0, M0+, M3, M4) or 8051 depending on family.
- ✅ **UDB = Universal Digital Block** — two PLD macrocells + 8-bit datapath + control registers.
- ✅ **PLD Macrocells** have **12 product terms** and **4 output terms** (similar to 22V10 PAL architecture).
- ✅ **Datapath ALU supports:** ADD, SUB, INC, DEC, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHIFT, CRC.
- ✅ **SAR ADC:** 12-bit, 1 Msps, used for fast conversions.
- ✅ **$\Delta\Sigma$ ADC:** up to 20-bit, used for high-resolution audio/sensor measurements.
- ✅ **ADC LSB formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$ (**not** $2^N$).
- ✅ **SNR formula:** $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76\,\text{dB}$.
- ✅ **ENOB formula:** $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$.
- ✅ **Sigma-delta oversampling formula:** $f_s = 2 \cdot f_{max} \cdot OSR$.
- ✅ **Sigma-delta resolution gain:** $\Delta N = 0.5 \cdot \log_2(OSR)$ for first-order; higher for higher-order.
- ✅ **Nyquist sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- ✅ **CapSense (CSD)** uses $\Delta\Sigma$ modulation for touch/proximity sensing.
- ✅ **Development Tools:** PSoC Creator, ModusToolbox (modern), with schematic capture + Verilog + C firmware.
- ✅ **Programming languages:** C/C++ for firmware, Verilog/VHDL for hardware description.
- ✅ **Power consumption:** as low as **20 nA** in deep-sleep mode.
- ✅ **Operating voltage:** typically **1.71 V to 5.5 V**.
- ✅ **PSoC differs from FPGA:** PSoC has built-in CPU + analog; FPGA does not.
- ✅ **PSoC differs from MCU:** PSoC peripherals are *configurable*; MCU peripherals are *fixed*.
- ✅ **Key applications:** CapSense touch UIs, sensor signal conditioning, motor control, IoT/BLE, USB peripherals.
- ✅ **DMA engine** allows peripheral-to-memory transfers without CPU intervention.
- ✅ **HSIOM** = High-Speed I/O Matrix for digital pin routing.
- ✅ **AG Bus** = Analog Global Bus; **AL Bus** = Analog Local Bus.
- ✅ **Successive Approximation** algorithm uses **binary search** to converge in $N$ clock cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When you see a 14-mark question on PSoC, always structure your answer as **(1) definition → (2) block diagram → (3) subsystem-by-subsystem explanation → (4) distinguishing feature → (5) one real-world example**. This structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and consistently scores 12-14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zynq So Design Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT412 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Zynq So Design Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Zynq-7000 System on Chip (SoC)** is a heterogeneous multi-core processing platform developed by **Xilinx (now AMD)** that tightly integrates a hard-wired **Processing System (PS)** based on the **dual-core ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore** with **7-series FPGA-based Programmable Logic (PL)** on a single silicon die. It is a true SoC — not a chip-level co-processor arrangement — where the PS and PL share a common die, common power rails, common clock network, and a high-bandwidth **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** interconnect fabric standardized under the **AMBA 4.0 specification**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEBT412):**
> Zynq SoCs are the de-facto reference platform taught in KTU's *Design of Logic Systems* course for understanding **hardware-software co-design**, because the same chip exposes both a **software-programmable** ARM side and a **hardware-reconfigurable** FPGA side, eliminating the traditional boundary between an ASIC and a discrete processor.

### 1.2 Key Architectural Entities

| Acronym | Expansion | Role in the SoC |
|---|---|---|
| **PS** | Processing System | Hard ARM Cortex-A9 subsystem (CPU, MMU, Cache, OCM, DDR Ctrl, I/O Peripherals) |
| **PL** | Programmable Logic | Xilinx 7-series FPGA fabric (LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP48E1, XADC, GTX Transceivers) |
| **AXI** | Advanced eXtensible Interface | AMBA 4.0 high-bandwidth on-chip bus linking PS ↔ PL |
| **MIO** | Multiplexed I/O | The **54 dedicated PS-side I/O pins** for peripherals (UART, I2C, SPI, USB, ENET) |
| **EMIO** | Extended MIO | A mechanism to route **PS peripherals out through the PL fabric** to PL-side package pins |
| **OCM** | On-Chip Memory | **256 KB** of dual-ported SRAM shared by both ARM cores and the PL |
| **ACP** | Accelerator Coherency Port | A **64-bit AXI slave** into the Cortex-A9 L1/L2 cache subsystem from PL |
| **HP** | High-Performance AXI | **64-bit** PL→PS paths to DDR & OCM with FIFO/clock-crossing |
| **GP** | General-Purpose AXI | **32-bit**, low-latency, control/status channels between PS & PL |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (for first-time learners):**
> Think of the Zynq-7000 chip as a **modern "Smart City" built on one plot of land**:
>
> 1. The **City Hall (PS)** is a permanent, stone-built administrative complex staffed by two experienced **bureaucrats (the dual ARM Cortex-A9 cores)**. They handle routine governance — running an OS, servicing citizens via standard counters (UART, USB, Ethernet, I²C, SPI), and managing the central water reservoir (**DDR3 memory**) and a small emergency tank (**OCM, 256 KB**).
> 2. The **Industrial District (PL)** is an empty warehouse lot next door, ready to be rented out and fitted with any kind of factory machinery the city needs — a high-speed number-cruncher, a custom video pipeline, a motor controller. You "build" the factory using FPGA logic gates and DSP blocks, then re-build it tomorrow if requirements change.
> 3. The **Highways (AXI Interconnect)** are a network of multi-lane expressways that connect City Hall to the Industrial District. Trucks (data words) move between them constantly. There are **four types of highways**: two high-speed freight corridors (**AXI HP**), two narrow service lanes (**AXI GP**), and one special expressway that lets factories talk *directly* to the City Hall's cache (**AXI ACP**) so the bureaucrats always see the freshest data without City Hall's warehouses getting involved.
> 4. The **Toll Booths (MIO / EMIO)** are the gates at the city's edge. The PS has its own **54 fixed toll booths (MIO)** for its standard services, and if a PL factory needs to talk to the outside world through a PS service (say, Ethernet), it must use a **virtual EMIO** lane that re-emerges inside the Industrial District and exits through a PL-owned toll booth.

This analogy captures the most important property of Zynq: **the PS is the always-on, software-defined master; the PL is the reconfigurable hardware accelerator that the PS can summon, supervise, and program on the fly.**

### 1.4 Why Zynq Matters in Logic System Design (KTU Context)

Traditional FPGA boards require an *external* microprocessor (a separate chip) to run high-level software. The Zynq SoC eliminates this by integrating everything onto one die, giving the designer:

- **Lower Bill of Materials (BoM)** — no separate MPU chip.
- **Lower Latency** — the AXI buses are on-chip, running at **up to 150 MHz** with **1 GB/s+ per HP port**.
- **Tighter Power Budget** — single voltage domain management (PS at **1.0 V core / 1.8 V–3.3 V I/O**, PL at **1.0 V core / user-selectable I/O**).
- **Heterogeneous Computing** — true HW/SW partitioning, where compute-intensive kernels (FFT, FIR, encryption) are pushed into PL and the control plane remains in PS software.

These are precisely the design trade-offs that KTU examiners test in the ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Processing System (PS) — Detailed Anatomy

The PS is a fully-featured application processor subsystem. It is composed of three major blocks:

1. **Application Processing Unit (APU)** — dual **ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore** with per-core:
   - **L1 cache**: 32 KB I-cache + 32 KB D-cache (Harvard, 4-way set associative).
   - **NEON SIMD** media engine and **single/double-precision VFPv3** floating-point unit.
   - **MMU** (Memory Management Unit) for virtual-to-physical address translation.
   - **GIC (Generic Interrupt Controller)** — supports up to **96 interrupt sources**.
2. **Memory Interfaces**:
   - **DDR controller** supporting **DDR3, DDR3L, LPDDR2, LPDDR3** (16-bit or 32-bit wide, up to **533 MHz / 1066 MT/s**).
   - **OCM** — **256 KB**, dual-ported, 4-way interleaved, supporting simultaneous CPU + PL access.
3. **I/O Peripherals** (all routed through MIO/EMIO):
   - **2x Tri-Mode Ethernet MAC** (10/100/1000 Mbps).
   - **2x USB 2.0 OTG** (Host + Device).
   - **2x SD/SDIO 2.0**.
   - **2x UART, 2x I²C, 2x SPI, 2x CAN 2.0B**.
   - **4x 32-bit GPIO** banks, **54 MIO pins** total.

### 2.2 The Programmable Logic (PL) — Detailed Anatomy

The PL is architecturally identical to a **Xilinx 7-series FPGA** (Artix-7 logic on Zynq-7010/7020; Kintex-7 logic on Zynq-7030/7045). It contains:

| Resource | Zynq-7010 | Zynq-7020 | Zynq-7030 | Zynq-7045 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Logic Cells | 28K | 85K | 125K | 350K |
| LUTs | 17,600 | 53,200 | 78,600 | 218,600 |
| Flip-Flops | 35,200 | 106,400 | 157,200 | 437,200 |
| Block RAM (36 Kb) | 60 | 140 | 265 | 545 |
| DSP Slices | 80 | 220 | 400 | 900 |
| Peak DSP Perf. | 100 GMACs | 276 GMACs | 593 GMACs | 1334 GMACs |

> **Source:** Xilinx Zynq-7000 datasheet (DS187, public). The "K" denotes thousands of logic cells.

The PL fabric is implemented in **28 nm HPL (High-Performance Low-power)** process technology and supports **1.0 V core voltage** (with optional **0.95 V** for low-power variants). It contains dedicated hard IP such as **GTX 6.25 Gb/s transceivers**, **XADC** (dual 12-bit 1 MSPS ADC), **PLLs (MMCM/PLL)**, and **PCIe Gen2** blocks (in larger Zynq devices).

### 2.3 The AXI Interconnect — The "Why" of High-Yield Bandwidth

The PS↔PL interconnect is the **architectural centerpiece** that KTU examiners love to question. It consists of **nine (9) AXI ports** organized into three categories:

```
                                  ┌──────────────────┐
   PL (Master)  ──► AXI HP[0..3] ─►│   DDR / OCM      │  (PL → PS memory)
   PL (Master)  ──► AXI GP[0]   ─►│   PS I/O Regs    │  (PL → PS control)
   PS (Master)  ──► AXI GP[1]   ─►│   PL Registers   │  (PS → PL)
   PL (Slave)   ◄── AXI ACP      ─│   Cortex-A9 L2   │  (PL → CPU cache)
   PL (Slave)   ◄── AXI HP[0..3] ─│   DDR Read Path  │  (PS memory → PL)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam-Critical Fact (highest weightage):**
> There are exactly **4 × AXI HP** ports (two full-duplex master + slave pairs), **2 × AXI GP** ports (one master + one slave), and **1 × AXI ACP** port. The 4+2+1+2 = **9 ports** total. This number is a recurring KTU question.

Each **AXI HP** port is **64-bit** wide, **clock-asynchronous** (PL and PS can be at different frequencies), and contains dedicated **FIFOs (1 KB each)** for write/read buffering, allowing the PL to stream data into DDR3 at up to **600 MB/s per port**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AXI HP Peak Bandwidth (per port) | $BW_{HP} \;=\; f_{AXI} \times W_{AXI} \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times 2$ (read+write) | $f_{AXI}\le 150$ MHz, $W_{AXI}=64$ bits |
| 2 | Aggregate HP Bandwidth (4 ports) | $BW_{4HP}\;\le\; 4 \times 600\;\text{MB/s}\;=\;2.4\;\text{GB/s}$ | Theoretical max |
| 3 | DDR3 Peak Throughput | $BW_{DDR}\;\le\; 2 \times f_{DDR} \times W_{DDR}$ | $f_{DDR}\le 533$ MHz, $W_{DDR}\in\{16,32\}$ |
| 4 | OCM Aggregate Bandwidth | $BW_{OCM}\;\le\; 2 \times 64\text{ bits} \times f_{CPU}$ | 128-bit aggregated, 4-way interleaved |
| 5 | Effective PL Logic Density | $\text{LUT}_{eff}\;=\;\text{LUTs}_{total}\times\eta_{util}$ | $\eta_{util}\approx 0.7$ post-PnR |
| 6 | DSP Slice MAC Throughput | $F_{MAC}\;=\;f_{DSP}\times N_{DSP}$ | $f_{DSP}\le 741$ MHz (DSP48E1) |
| 7 | Zynq Power Budget Rule | $P_{total}\;\approx\;P_{PS,active}+P_{PL,dyn}+P_{I/O}$ | $P_{PS}\approx 1.5$–$2.5$ W (typ.) |
| 8 | ACP Cache Coherency Latency | $\tau_{ACP}\;\approx\;4$–$8$ $f_{CPU}$ cycles | Without L1/L2 contention |
| 9 | Boot Time (BootROM + FSBL) | $t_{boot}\;\approx\;20$–$250$ ms | Depends on boot medium (NAND/NOR/QSPI/SD) |
| 10 | MIO Pin Count | $N_{MIO}\;=\;54$ | Fixed, banked at 1.8 V or 2.5 V / 3.3 V |

> **Pitfall note for KTU valuation:** When asked for *peak* bandwidth, students often forget the **×2 multiplier** for full-duplex. Forgetting this is a guaranteed **1-mark** loss.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Zynq SoC has become the de-facto platform for:

- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** — PS runs Linux + baseband, PL does FFT/fir/decim.
- **Motor Control & Industrial Drives** — PS runs control loop math (MATLAB/Simulink-generated), PL handles high-resolution PWM generation.
- **Medical Imaging** — PS hosts the UI and patient database, PL performs real-time ultrasound/FFT/convolution.
- **Automotive ADAS & Driver Monitoring** — PL accelerates image preprocessing, PS runs ML inference (e.g., CNN for object detection).

This is why KTU places it inside the *Design of Logic Systems* course: it is the natural endpoint of any digital logic design — turning a finite-state machine into a real, OS-controllable, end-to-end product.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Sustainable Data Throughput Between PL and DDR

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style data):**
A Zynq-7020 has its PL generating 32-bit video pixel data at $f_{PIX}=100$ MHz. The data must be written to DDR3 through one AXI HP port. Compute the maximum throughput and check whether DDR3 can absorb the stream.

**Step 1 — Compute the raw data rate from the PL source.**

$$
R_{src} \;=\; f_{PIX} \times W_{PIX} \times \eta_{pack}
$$

where $W_{PIX}=32$ bits, $\eta_{pack}=1$ (full packing), and $f_{PIX}=100$ MHz.

$$
R_{src} \;=\; 100 \times 10^{6} \;\times\; 32\;\text{bits} \;\times\; 1 \;=\; 3.2\;\text{Gbps}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
R_{src,bytes} \;=\; \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{9}}{8} \;=\; 400\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-port AXI HP capacity.**

$$
BW_{HP} \;=\; f_{AXI} \times W_{AXI,bytes}
$$

Using the maximum $f_{AXI}=150$ MHz and $W_{AXI,bytes}=8$ bytes (64 bits):

$$
BW_{HP,write} \;=\; 150 \times 10^{6} \;\times\; 8 \;=\; 1.2\;\text{GB/s}
$$

This is the **theoretical ceiling** for a single port. The *sustainable* throughput is typically **70–80 %** of the theoretical ceiling after AXI protocol overhead (AW/W channel, R/B responses, arbitration):

$$
BW_{HP,sus} \;\approx\; 0.75 \times 1.2 \;\text{GB/s} \;\approx\; 900\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with DDR3 capacity.**

The DDR3 controller in Zynq-7000 can run up to **533 MHz (DDR)** with a **32-bit** data width:

$$
BW_{DDR,max} \;=\; 2 \times 533 \times 10^{6} \;\times\; 4 \;\text{bytes} \;\approx\; 4.264\;\text{GB/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check feasibility.**

$$
R_{src,bytes} \;=\; 400\;\text{MB/s} \;\ll\; BW_{HP,sus}\;\approx\;900\;\text{MB/s} \;\ll\; BW_{DDR,max}
$$

> **Conclusion (KTU-style answer line):**
> The 100 MHz × 32-bit video stream is comfortably absorbed by *one* AXI HP port, with the DDR3 having a 10× margin. The design is feasible; no port aggregation is required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — PS Boot Sequence and Latency Stacking

The Zynq boots in a deterministic, multi-stage sequence. A KTU problem may ask: *"Given a 4 MB FSBL stored in Quad-SPI flash with $t_{QRead}=40$ ns per 4-byte word, what is the minimum boot time?"*

**Step 1 — BootROM stage (always-on mask ROM).**
The BootROM is a hard-coded mask ROM inside the PS. It reads the **Boot Mode pins (BOOT_MODE[4:0])** and configures the boot device controller accordingly. This stage consumes approximately **10 ms** of fixed time ($t_{BR}$).

**Step 2 — Transfer the FSBL to OCM.**

The FSBL is fetched from the boot device. For Quad-SPI at $f_{QSPI}=100$ MHz (x4 IO mode):

$$
R_{QSPI} \;=\; f_{QSPI} \times 4 \;\text{IO-lines} \times \dfrac{1\;\text{byte}}{2\;\text{cycles}}\;\text{(DDR)}
$$

Wait — for SDR Quad-SPI:

$$
R_{QSPI} \;=\; 100 \;\text{MHz} \;\times\; 4\;\text{bits} \;\times\; \dfrac{1}{2} \;=\; 200\;\text{MB/s}
$$

For our 4 MB FSBL:

$$
t_{FSBL,fetch} \;=\; \dfrac{4\;\text{MB}}{200\;\text{MB/s}} \;=\; 20\;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — FSBL execution.**
The FSBL is a small Cortex-A9 program that initializes PS clocks, DDR, peripherals, then loads the user **Bitstream (for PL)** and **Application (for PS)**. Its execution time is design-dependent, but a typical figure is **$t_{FSBL,exec}\approx 50$–$100$ ms**.

**Step 4 — Bitstream load (PL configuration).**

The PL bitstream size is device-dependent. For Zynq-7020, a typical partial bitstream might be 3.5 MB:

$$
t_{BIT,load} \;=\; \dfrac{3.5\;\text{MB}}{200\;\text{MB/s}} \;\approx\; 17.5\;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Total boot time (latency stack).**

$$
t_{boot,total} \;=\; t_{BR} \;+\; t_{FSBL,fetch} \;+\; t_{FSBL,exec} \;+\; t_{BIT,load} \;+\; t_{APP,load}
$$

$$
t_{boot,total} \;\approx\; 10 \;+\; 20 \;+\; 75 \;+\; 17.5 \;+\; 30 \;\approx\; 152.5\;\text{ms}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A Zynq-7020 board can reach "Linux prompt" in approximately **150–250 ms** from a properly laid-out QSPI flash, while complex Linux boots (with full device tree + rootfs) typically extend this to **2–4 seconds**. KTU problems usually target the **stage-by-stage breakdown** rather than absolute figures.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Interrupt Latency in the GIC (Generic Interrupt Controller)

A common KTU question is the maximum latency from an PL-side interrupt to ARM-core response.

**Step 1 — Interrupt assertion propagation (PL fabric to PS IRQ input).**
The PL drives an `IRQF2P[n]` line (where $n \in [0..15]$ for fabric-generated interrupts). Routing delay through the PL interconnect and across the die to the GIC:

$$
t_{prop,PL} \;\approx\; 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cycles of } f_{CPU}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — GIC prioritization and distribution.**

$$
t_{GIC,dist} \;\approx\; 12 \text{ to } 20 \text{ cycles of } f_{CPU}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — CPU context save and ISR entry.**

$$
t_{entry} \;\approx\; 8 \text{ to } 12 \text{ cycles of } f_{CPU}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Total worst-case latency at $f_{CPU}=667$ MHz (Zynq-7020 max).**

$$
T_{cycle} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{667 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 1.5\;\text{ns}
$$

$$
t_{IRQ,total} \;\approx\; (4 + 20 + 12) \times 1.5\;\text{ns} \;\approx\; 54\;\text{ns}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Zynq offers **sub-microsecond** interrupt response — this is one of the chief engineering reasons a Zynq is preferred over a discrete CPU+FPGA pair, where such latency is typically **hundreds of nanoseconds** due to off-chip PCB trace delays.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Interconnect for Common Workloads

| Workload | Best PS↔PL Path | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Stream pixel/ADC data into DDR | **AXI HP (PL→DDR)** | Highest bandwidth, FIFO-buffered, clock-async |
| Stream processed data from DDR to PL DSP | **AXI HP (DDR→PL)** | Symmetric, full-duplex HP pair |
| Tight cache-coupled accelerator (e.g., custom CPU) | **AXI ACP** | Hardware cache-coherent with Cortex-A9 L2 |
| Register-level status/control (PS reads PL state) | **AXI GP** | Low-latency, 32-bit, ideal for control planes |
| Program PL-side register from PS (e.g., DMA engine config) | **AXI GP** | One master, deterministic |
| EMIO-driven slow peripheral (PS-side UART routed through PL pins) | **EMIO** | Reuses PS peripheral, expands pin choice |
| Direct PL → CPU interrupt (e.g., "data ready" signal) | **IRQF2P[n] + GIC** | Hard-wired, sub-100 ns latency |

> **Exam tip:** The most common KTU mistake is using **AXI GP** for high-bandwidth streaming. GP ports are intended for **control/status registers**, not data paths. The exam penalty is usually **2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Zynq-7000 SoC Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical Zynq-7000 top-level architecture. The **Mermaid ID-Alpha Rule** is followed (no reserved keywords; all IDs are alphanumeric and start with a letter).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PS["PROCESSING SYSTEM (PS) - ARM Cortex-A9"]
        CPU1["CPU0 Cortex-A9 MPCore"]
        CPU2["CPU1 Cortex-A9 MPCore"]
        L1C["L1 I-Cache / D-Cache 32KB+32KB per core"]
        L2C["Shared L2 Cache 512KB"]
        MMU["MMU and GIC Interrupt Controller"]
        OCM["OCM 256KB Dual-Port SRAM"]
        DDRC["DDR3 Controller 16 or 32-bit, up to 1066 MT/s"]
        PERIPH["I/O Peripherals: UART, I2C, SPI, USB, ENET, CAN, SD, GPIO"]
    end

    subgraph INTERCONNECT["AXI INTERCONNECT FABRIC (AMBA 4.0)"]
        AXIHP["AXI HP x4 64-bit, 150 MHz, async, FIFO-buffered"]
        AXIGP["AXI GP x2 32-bit, control/status"]
        AXIACP["AXI ACP 64-bit, cache-coherent to L2"]
    end

    subgraph PL["PROGRAMMABLE LOGIC (PL) - 7-Series FPGA"]
        FPGA["FPGA Fabric: LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP48E1"]
        GTX["GTX Transceivers 6.25 Gbps"]
        XADC["XADC Dual 12-bit 1 MSPS"]
        PCIE["PCIe Gen2 Hard Block"]
    end

    subgraph BOOT["BOOT AND CLOCK SUBSYSTEM"]
        BOOTROM["BootROM Mask ROM"]
        CLOCK["PS Clock: 33.333 MHz Crystal x3 PLLs"]
        MIO["MIO 54 pins for PS peripherals"]
        EMIO["EMIO Virtual routing through PL"]
    end

    CPU1 --> L1C --> L2C
    CPU2 --> L1C
    L2C --> MMU
    L2C --> AXIACP
    L2C --> OCM
    OCM --> DDRC
    PERIPH --> MIO
    PERIPH --> EMIO
    BOOTROM --> CPU1
    CLOCK --> PS
    CLOCK --> PL

    AXIHP --> DDRC
    AXIHP --> OCM
    AXIGP --> PERIPH
    AXIACP --> L2C

    DDRC -. "AXI HP write/read" .-> AXIHP
    OCM -. "AXI HP write/read" .-> AXIHP
    PERIPH -. "AXI GP control" .-> AXIGP
    FPGA -. "AXI ACP cache-coherent" .-> AXIACP

    AXIHP -- "PL to DDR" --> FPGA
    AXIGP -- "PS to PL control" --> FPGA
    FPGA --> GTX
    FPGA --> XADC
    FPGA --> PCIE
    EMIO --> FPGA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The *Processing System* lives on the left in a hard-wired ARM cluster, the *Programmable Logic* on the right as reconfigurable FPGA fabric, and the *AXI Interconnect Fabric* in the middle acts as the **multi-lane highway system** that the two halves use to communicate. Note the three different AXI port classes — **HP** (high bandwidth to DDR/OCM), **GP** (control/status), **ACP** (cache-coherent accelerator port).

### 4.2 MIO vs EMIO Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START(["PS Peripheral needs external pin"]) --> Q1{"Is pin available\nin MIO 54?"}
    Q1 -- "YES" --> A1["Use MIO\nfixed PS pin"]
    Q1 -- "NO" --> A2["Use EMIO\nroute through PL pin"]
    A2 --> Q2{"PL fabric\nresource sufficient?"}
    Q2 -- "YES" --> A3["Proceed with EMIO\n+ PL pin assignment"]
    Q2 -- "NO" --> A4["Re-plan\nreduce PL usage"]

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style A3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style A4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

> **Rule of thumb (often asked in KTU):** *MIO* is always preferred when the pin is available, because it bypasses the PL fabric and saves LUTs/routing. *EMIO* is used only when **(a)** the MIO bank is full, or **(b)** the peripheral is required but the device variant has fewer MIO pins, or **(c)** the peripheral signal must be combined with PL-side logic before reaching the package pin (e.g., an interrupt-conditioned GPIO).

### 4.3 Zynq Design Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Step 1: System Specification\nPartition HW versus SW"] --> B["Step 2: PL Design in Vivado HLS or RTL"]
    B --> C["Step 3: PS Configuration in Vivado\nZynq Block Design + IP Integrator"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Generate Bitstream\nPL configuration file .bit"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Export Hardware\n.xsa to Vitis or SDK"]
    E --> F["Step 6: PS Firmware in C/C++\nBare-metal or Linux driver"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Build FSBL\nFirst Stage Boot Loader"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Package Boot Image\nBOOT.bin = FSBL + bitstream + app"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Flash to QSPI or SD card"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Board Power-On\nZynq boots and runs application"]

    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define a *Zynq SoC*. What are its two major functional blocks and how do they communicate?

**Model Answer (3 marks — examiner allocation):**

A Zynq SoC (System on Chip) is a single integrated circuit that combines a **hard-wired Processing System (PS)** with a **reconfigurable Programmable Logic (PL)** on the same silicon die. (1 mark)

- The **PS** consists of a dual-core ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore, L1/L2 caches, MMU, GIC, OCM (256 KB), DDR controller, and standard I/O peripherals (UART, I²C, SPI, USB, Ethernet). (1 mark)
- The **PL** is a 7-series FPGA fabric containing LUTs, flip-flops, Block RAM, DSP slices, GTX transceivers, and PCIe hard IP. (½ mark)
- They communicate via a high-bandwidth **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface) interconnect** consisting of 4 AXI HP ports, 2 AXI GP ports, and 1 AXI ACP port, all compliant with the AMBA 4.0 specification. (½ mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between MIO and EMIO in a Zynq-7000 SoC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **MIO (Multiplexed I/O)** | **EMIO (Extended MIO)** |
|---|---|---|
| Physical location | PS-side dedicated pins | Routed through PL fabric to PL pins |
| Pin count | 54 fixed | Depends on PL package pin count |
| Use case | When pin count fits within 54 | When MIO banks are saturated or pin-muxing needed |
| Resource cost | No PL resources used | Consumes PL routing & I/O buffers |
| Latency | Lowest (direct path) | Slightly higher (PL fabric delay) |

(1½ marks for definition each, 1 mark for the comparative table summary)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks, Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **Zynq-7000 SoC**. Clearly identify the PS, PL, and all AXI interconnect paths.

**Model Solution (7 marks allocation):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define Zynq as an SoC with PS + PL on a single die using 28 nm HPL process.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the **PS** — Dual ARM Cortex-A9 cores, each with 32 KB I-cache + 32 KB D-cache, shared 512 KB L2 cache, NEON + FPU engines, MMU, GIC, 256 KB OCM, DDR3 controller, and peripherals (UART, USB, ENET, I²C, SPI, CAN, SD, GPIO). State the clock domain (typically 666 MHz for CPU, 533 MHz for DDR).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Describe the **PL** — 7-series FPGA fabric, with LUTs, FFs, BRAM (36 Kb), DSP48E1 slices, GTX transceivers (6.25 Gbps), XADC, MMCM/PLL clock tiles, and PCIe Gen2 hard block. Provide the resource counts for one of the variants (e.g., Zynq-7020: 85 K logic cells, 220 DSP slices).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Describe the **AXI Interconnect** with all **9 ports**:
- **4 × AXI HP** (High Performance) — 64-bit, PL↔DDR/OCM, FIFO-buffered, asynchronous clocks.
- **2 × AXI GP** (General Purpose) — 32-bit, PS↔PL control/status.
- **1 × AXI ACP** (Accelerator Coherency Port) — 64-bit, cache-coherent access to L2.
- **2 × AXI HP slave** ports (read-back paths from DDR to PL).
Draw a labelled block diagram showing these paths (this is essential for full marks).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **boot sequence** of a Zynq-7000 SoC with a QSPI flash as the boot device. Compute the **minimum boot time** for a 2 MB FSBL and a 3 MB bitstream, given Quad-SPI x4 at 100 MHz.

**Model Solution (7 marks allocation):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Explain the multi-stage boot:
1. **Stage 0 — BootROM** (hard mask ROM, ~10 ms): reads BOOT_MODE pins, configures the boot device controller (here, Quad-SPI).
2. **Stage 1 — FSBL** (First Stage Boot Loader, user-supplied): initializes PS clocks, MIO, DDR, and PL.
3. **Stage 2 — Bitstream + Application** loaded by FSBL into PL and DDR respectively.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute QSPI bandwidth.
At 100 MHz × 4 IO lines in SDR mode:
$$R_{QSPI} = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \text{ bits} \times \frac{1}{2} = 200 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute FSBL fetch time.
$$t_{FSBL} = \frac{2 \text{ MB}}{200 \text{ MB/s}} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute bitstream load time and total.
$$t_{BIT} = \frac{3 \text{ MB}}{200 \text{ MB/s}} = 15 \text{ ms}$$

Adding BootROM overhead (~10 ms) and FSBL execution overhead (~50 ms typical):

$$t_{boot,total} \approx 10 + 10 + 50 + 15 \approx 85 \text{ ms (minimum)}$$

> **Examiner's note (full marks):** Mention the two file formats — `BOOT.bin` (FSBL + bitstream + app) and the BIF (Boot Image Format) file that combines them. Also note that a Linux build with full rootfs can extend this to **2–4 s**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **AXI HP**, **AXI GP**, and **AXI ACP** ports in a Zynq-7000 SoC. For each, give the data width, typical use case, and bandwidth.

**Model Solution (7 marks allocation):**

| Parameter | **AXI HP** | **AXI GP** | **AXI ACP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data width | **64-bit** | **32-bit** | **64-bit** |
| Number of ports | **4 (2 master + 2 slave)** | **2 (1 master + 1 slave)** | **1 (slave only)** |
| Direction | Full-duplex PL↔PS | PS→PL & PL→PS | PL→PS L2 cache only |
| Typical use | High-bandwidth streaming (video, ADC, DMA) | Register-level control & status | Tightly-coupled cache-coherent accelerator |
| Peak bandwidth per port | ~1.2 GB/s read or write | ~600 MB/s | Coherent w/ L2 latency 4–8 cycles |
| Asynchronous clocks | Yes (FIFO-buffered) | Synchronous to PS | Synchronous to PS |
| Coherency | Non-coherent (manual flush required) | Non-coherent | **Hardware cache-coherent** |

(Marks: 1 for the table, 1 for use case elaboration, 1 for the coherency distinction — which is the *key* concept)

**Exam-critical concept (½ mark):** ACP is the *only* port that gives the PL *hardware* cache coherency with the Cortex-A9 L2. Without ACP, the software must manually invalidate/flush the L1/L2 cache lines when sharing data with PL — this is error-prone and slow.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Zynq-7020 PL generates 16-bit ADC samples at $f_{s}=200$ MHz. The data must be written to DDR3 via an AXI HP port. Determine:
- (i) The required AXI HP clock frequency if the AXI link is fully utilized.
- (ii) The number of AXI HP ports needed if the sustainable throughput is 70 % of the theoretical maximum.
- (iii) Suggest an alternative if a single port is insufficient.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Source data rate:**
$$R_{src} = f_s \times W = 200 \times 10^{6} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$$

For one AXI HP port with $W_{AXI}=64$ bits = 8 bytes:
$$f_{AXI,min} = \frac{R_{src}}{W_{AXI,bytes}} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 50 \text{ MHz}$$

So $f_{AXI} \geq 50$ MHz is sufficient *in theory*. (2 marks)

**(ii) Sustainable throughput per port at 70 % efficiency:**

If $f_{AXI}$ is set to 150 MHz (the maximum):
$$BW_{HP,max} = 150 \times 10^{6} \times 8 = 1.2 \text{ GB/s}$$
$$BW_{HP,sus} = 0.7 \times 1.2 = 840 \text{ MB/s}$$

Since $R_{src}=400$ MB/s $< 840$ MB/s, **one port is sufficient** at 70 % efficiency. (2 marks)

**(iii) Alternatives (3 marks):**
1. **Use a wider internal data path in PL** — pack two 16-bit samples into one 32-bit word before issuing the AXI write, halving the AXI transactions.
2. **Aggregate two AXI HP ports** — guaranteed headroom.
3. **Use the PL-side BRAM as a temporary FIFO** — decouples the PL producer from AXI bursts, allowing the AXI port to write in large bursts (256-beat) to maximize efficiency.
4. **Reduce sample rate** if signal bandwidth allows decimation.

> **Alternative final remark (½ mark):** For continuous very-high-rate streams (e.g., multi-GSPS ADCs), the **GTX transceivers** with external memory would be a more appropriate channel than AXI HP.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 — Zynq SoC):**
> 1. **Confusing the AXI port count.** Students frequently write "4 HP + 2 GP + 1 ACP = 7" — forgetting that HP has *separate read and write* sub-channels, so the total *AXI channels* are 4+2+1 = 7, but the *AXI port instances* are still 4+2+1 = 7 *functionally*. The cleanest answer is: **9 physical ports** (4 HP + 2 GP + 1 ACP + 2 GP extensions via EMIO) or simply stating "AXI HP (4) + AXI GP (2) + AXI ACP (1)" — examiners accept the "(4+2+1) = 7 functional classes" framing.
> 2. **Mixing up the 256 KB and 512 KB figures.** OCM is **256 KB**; L2 cache is **512 KB**. This is a **2-mark trap** in viva.
> 3. **Calling PL "the ARM side".** The PL is the *FPGA fabric*, not the ARM side. This terminology error costs **1 mark** and signals weak understanding.
> 4. **Forgetting the AXI HP port width of 64 bits.** Students sometimes write "32-bit HP" — that is the **GP** width, not HP. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not mentioning AMBA 4.0 / AXI3/AXI4 spec.** The *protocol* name "AXI" without the bus-protocol context loses a mark — Zynq-7000 uses **AXI3** (HP/GP) and **AXI4-Lite** (control).
> 6. **Omitting the clock-frequency limit of 150 MHz for AXI HP.** Examiners look for this in the bandwidth calculation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Zynq SoC Design Overview):**

- **Definition:** Zynq-7000 = **dual ARM Cortex-A9 PS** + **7-series FPGA PL** on a single 28 nm die, made by Xilinx (AMD).
- **PS contains:** Dual Cortex-A9, 32 KB + 32 KB L1/core, **512 KB L2**, 256 KB OCM, DDR3 ctrl (≤ 1066 MT/s), 2× USB 2.0, 2× GigE, 2× SD, 2× UART/I²C/SPI/CAN, 4× GPIO, **54 MIO pins**.
- **PL contains:** 7-series FPGA fabric (LUTs, FFs, BRAM 36 Kb, DSP48E1), GTX 6.25 Gbps, XADC 1 MSPS, PCIe Gen2, MMCM/PLL.
- **AXI Interconnect has 9 ports:** 4× **HP** (64-bit, async FIFO), 2× **GP** (32-bit, control), 1× **ACP** (64-bit, cache-coherent to L2).
- **MIO** = 54 fixed PS-side pins. **EMIO** = virtual PS-peripheral route through PL fabric.
- **Boot sequence:** BootROM (10 ms) → FSBL (from QSPI/SD/NAND) → Bitstream → Application. Stored as `BOOT.bin`.
- **Toolchain:** Vivado Design Suite (PL), Vitis (PS firmware, formerly SDK), Petalinux (Linux), Vivado HLS (C-to-FPGA).
- **Key numbers to memorize:**
  - MIO pins = **54**
  - OCM = **256 KB**
  - L2 cache = **512 KB**
  - L1 per core = **32 KB I + 32 KB D**
  - AXI HP width = **64 bits**
  - AXI GP width = **32 bits**
  - AXI HP clock = **≤ 150 MHz**
  - DDR3 clock = **≤ 533 MHz (1066 MT/s)**
  - GTX rate = **6.25 Gbps**
  - BootROM source clock = **33.333 MHz crystal** → PS PLL → 666/533/150 MHz
- **Bandwidth rules of thumb:**
  - Single AXI HP peak = **1.2 GB/s** (150 MHz × 8 B).
  - Four HP aggregated = **~2.4 GB/s** theoretical.
  - DDR3 peak (32-bit) = **~4.26 GB/s** (533 MHz DDR × 4 B).
- **Interrupt latency:** Sub-microsecond, typically **~50–100 ns** from PL `IRQF2P[n]` to ARM ISR entry.
- **Design flow:** Spec → Vivado PL → Zynq Block Design → Bitstream → Vitis FSBL → BOOT.bin → Flash → Boot.
- **KTU mantra:** *"PS is the software-defined brain, PL is the hardware-defined muscle, AXI is the nervous system."*
